sections.header: Verses 1–23 — More Judahite Clans; Jabez’s Prayer

heb.paragraph: Jabez’s name means “pain” — his mother named him for her difficult labour (v.9). His prayer asks God to reverse the meaning of his name: expand my territory, be with me, keep me from harm so I cause no pain. God grants it. Buried in a genealogy, this two-verse prayer became one of the most famous passages in Chronicles.

refs.note: Rachel named her dying son Ben-Oni (“son of my trouble”); Jacob renamed him Benjamin (“son of my right hand”). Jabez’s prayer similarly seeks to overcome a name of pain.
“Ask and it will be given to you.” Jabez asks; God gives. The pattern is simple and universal.

notes.note: Jabez’s prayer teaches that God is accessible to anyone who calls upon him. No special status, no priestly mediation — a man whose very name means “pain” receives God’s full blessing through simple, direct prayer. Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The name yaʿbēts is related to ʿōtseb/ʿetseb (“pain, sorrow”). The wordplay: “she named him Jabez (Pain), saying, ‘I gave birth to him in pain.’” His prayer asks God to overturn the destiny encoded in his name. The NET translators note that the underlying Hebrew/Greek syntax carries nuances that most English versions cannot fully represent. Lexical analysis of the key terms reveals semantic ranges that enrich the passage beyond what a single English gloss can convey, connecting this text to a wider web of biblical usage.

hist.context: The Judahite genealogies continue with additional clans, including the famous Jabez prayer (vv.9–10). In the middle of a list of names, the narrator pauses for two verses: Jabez prays, and God grants his request. Then the genealogy resumes as if nothing happened. The effect is striking — a flash of personal devotion in an institutional document. The Chronicler includes it because it illustrates his central theme: those who seek God find him. The genealogy then moves to Simeon’s tribe, with territorial notes about expansion into Edomite territory.

notes.note: The priority of prayer in the life of God's people cannot be overstated. Scripture consistently shows that God responds to the humble, dependent prayer of His people. This passage reminds believers that prayer is not merely a religious duty but the divinely appointed means by which God's purposes are accomplished through His people (Phil 4:6-7).

Authentic worship is not merely external ritual but the expression of a heart wholly devoted to God. The elaborate ceremonies described here were meaningful only insofar as they reflected genuine internal devotion. God has always desired obedience over sacrifice (1 Sam 15:22), and worship in spirit and truth over mere religious performance (John 4:23-24).

The Chronicler wrote for a post-exilic community needing to understand their identity and hope. His message remains vital: God's promises to David are irrevocable, worship is the center of covenant life, and faithfulness — not circumstances — determines blessing. Believers today can take the same comfort: our God is faithful even when we are not (2 Tim 2:13).

sections.header: Verses 24–43 — Simeon’s Descendants and Territorial Expansion

refs.note: Jacob’s prophecy: Simeon and Levi will be scattered in Israel. Simeon’s absorption into Judah fulfils this prediction.
Simeon’s allotment was within Judah’s territory — the smallest tribal inheritance.

notes.note: “To this day” links the past to the present. The Chronicler writes not as an antiquarian but as a witness: these genealogies describe people who are still living in these places. History is not over. Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The “Hamites” (hammĕʿûnîm or hammāʿōnîm) living in Gedor are otherwise unknown. The text may be corrupt; some LXX manuscripts read “Gerar” instead of “Gedor,” which would place the expansion in the Negev. The NET translators note that the underlying Hebrew/Greek syntax carries nuances that most English versions cannot fully represent. Lexical analysis of the key terms reveals semantic ranges that enrich the passage beyond what a single English gloss can convey, connecting this text to a wider web of biblical usage.

hist.context: Simeon’s genealogy is brief compared to Judah’s, reflecting the tribe’s small size and eventual absorption into Judah. The Chronicler notes that Simeonites expanded into Gedor and Mount Seir during Hezekiah’s reign, dispossessing the Hamites and Amalekites. This territorial note connects the genealogy to lived history: names map onto land, and land claims persist across generations.

heb.paragraph: The Hebrew tôlĕḏôt (generations) is a structuring device that traces the thread of God's purpose through family lines. From Genesis to Chronicles, genealogies are not mere lists but theological arguments: they demonstrate continuity, establish legitimacy, and show that God's covenant promises pass from generation to generation.

notes.note: These genealogical records are not mere ancient census data but the sovereign hand of God tracing His redemptive purposes through specific family lines. Every name preserved here testifies to God's faithfulness in maintaining the covenant lineage that would ultimately produce the Messiah (Matt 1:1-17; Luke 3:23-38).

God's meticulous preservation of these genealogical records demonstrates His commitment to His covenantal promises. Each generation listed is a link in the chain of redemptive history, connecting the patriarchal promises to their ultimate fulfillment in Christ. God does not forget His Word, even across centuries of human failure.

Believers today are grafted into this same covenant lineage through faith in Christ (Gal 3:29). The God who faithfully preserved His people through these generations is the same God who preserves His church today. No purpose of God can be thwarted (Job 42:2), and no believer truly in Christ will be lost (John 10:28-29).

These were the descendants of Hur, the firstborn of Ephrathah and father of Bethlehem.ESVand Penuel fathered Gedor, and Ezer fathered Hushah. These were the sons of Hur, the firstborn of Ephrathah, the father of Bethlehem.
